data.title: Artisan Global Discovery Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investing Environment

Global equities continued to climb in Q3, finishing the period with double-digit year-to-date gains. Major indices, including the MSCI ACWI, Russell 1000® and MSCI EAFE, reached record highs.

Rate cuts by global central banks helped support equity markets. Weaker US labor market data and Fed Chair Jerome Powell's remarks at the Jackson Hole Symposium regarding the shifting balance of economic risks and monetary policy in restrictive territory raised expectations of a rate cut. The Fed followed through with a 25bps rate cut at its September monetary policy meeting. Central banks in several other countries, including the UK, Canada and Australia, cut rates in Q3.

Equity market gains were supported by solid quarterly earnings results and the investment boom in artificial intelligence (AI). Through the first half of the year, AI-related capital expenditures contributed more than 1% to US GDP growth, and tech investment is growing at the fastest pace since 2000. It was no surprise then that information technology and communication services were leading sector performers. At the individual stock level, however, many of the stronger performing technology-related names were lower quality (i.e., unprofitable, low ROE, high beta).

Tariff concerns eased during the period as the US reached trade agreements with Japan, the European Union and South Korea. While negotiations remained unresolved with other major trading partners, including China, Mexico and Canada, tensions did not escalate.

A notable market change during the quarter was the performance of small-cap stocks. The Russell 2000® Index reached its first record high since 2021 and outpaced its US mid- and large-cap peers. This marked a reversal from the first half of the year, when large-cap stocks outperformed. Outside the US, the MSCI World ex USA Small Cap Index also outpaced its mid- and large-cap counterparts.

At the same time, large-cap growth stocks resumed their outperformance against value stocks. However, value fared better among small and mid caps. US markets generally outperformed other developed markets, while emerging markets beat developed markets, including the US. China, Korea and Japan contributed significantly to Asia's strong regional performance, aided by strength in IT spending, reduced trade tensions and a weaker US dollar.

Performance Discussion

The portfolio generated a modestly positive absolute return in Q3, but it trailed the MSCI All Country World Small Mid Index. The Q3 underperformance was driven by stock selection in the IT, financials and consumer discretionary sectors.

Amid the market excitement surrounding AI, our semiconductor, hardware and equipment names, such as Pure Storage (PSTG), Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC) and Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR), were strong contributors to the portfolio's relative performance. However, our software industry holdings, including Tyler Technologies (TYL) and Atlassian (TEAM), were a significant drag. Given the rapid advancements in AI capabilities, investors have become more uncertain about which software and services companies can successfully integrate AI into their products and which will have their business models severely disrupted.

Within financials, recent market and economic conditions have been more favorable for banks and other businesses that are credit-sensitive and spread-based. However, as a quality-focused growth manager, these are businesses that just won't typically meet our investment criteria.

On the positive side, stock selection in the health care and industrials sectors was the strongest contributor to performance. Some of the earlier fears over pharmaceutical tariffs and US drug price controls appear to have moderated. Still, the success within health care, the portfolio's largest sector exposure, was driven by strong company-specific results, particularly among our biotech holdings.

Health care is still not a popular sector for investors, hindered by policy uncertainty and understandably overshadowed by investor fascination with all things AI. However, we always say that where there's innovation, you tend to find profit cycles. And plenty of innovation is still occurring within health care.

Among our top contributors were Modine Manufacturing (MOD), Pure Storage and Ascendis Pharma. Modine is a global leader in thermal management solutions. The company is experiencing strong growth, driven by increased business with existing hyperscale customers and the addition of new accounts in the hyperscale segment. Rising demand across newer geographies, particularly in the US and Southeast Asia, and Modine's expansion into advanced product lines, such as rack-level and modular data center cooling solutions, have further fueled this momentum. As a result, the company has significantly raised its revenue expectations for the year.

Pure Storage is redefining enterprise data storage by sourcing raw NAND flash memory and building custom all-flash systems, which enhances cost efficiency. The company is gaining share in core enterprise markets, and its collaboration with SK hynix for hyperscale data center environments supports its efforts to expand its addressable market. During the quarter, Pure Storage reported revenue rose 13% year over year to $861 million, driven by enterprise demand and strong performance in its subscription services and next-generation product portfolio. While cloud and hyperscaler traction are still early, core business metrics remain solid.

Ascendis is a biotech company leveraging its proprietary TransCon™ platform to develop differentiated therapies that address unmet medical needs. This technology enables sustained and predictable drug release while preserving the drug's original mechanism of action. The launch of Yorvpath®—the first and only hormone replacement therapy for adult hypoparathyroidism—has exceeded expectations, generating revenues of €103 million in Q2 2025, more than double the prior quarter, with over 3,100 patients and 1,500 US prescribers. This strong uptake reinforces our confidence in both Yorvpath® and Ascendis' broader pipeline.

Among our Q3 detractors were London Stock Exchange Group (LNSTY), PROCEPT BioRobotics (PRCT) and Wingstop (WING). London Stock Exchange Group (LSEG) is a leading global provider of market infrastructure and capital markets services. We are attracted to its ability to further monetize its proprietary data, particularly through its early partnership with Microsoft (MSFT). During the quarter, competitive pressures intensified in the Workstation segment, with rivals—especially FactSet—cutting prices aggressively. While we believe LSEG has responded well and strengthened its offering from a lower price point, these pressures have created short-term headwinds. Additionally, the emergence of advanced AI models, such as Anthropic's Claude for Financial Services, has raised questions about potential disruption to financial information services. However, we view these large language models more as new distribution channels for LSEG's proprietary data than as threats to its core business. Despite our continued confidence in the company's long-term outlook over the next 24 months, these near-term challenges have led us to trim the position.

PROCEPT BioRobotics develops robotic systems for treating benign prostatic hyperplasia and is rapidly expanding in a largely underpenetrated market, supported by strong clinical evidence, improving insurance coverage and superior outcomes. Shares fell despite reporting 55% year-over-year revenue growth and rising margins, due to the unexpected retirement of its CEO. We view the appointment of new CEO Larry Wood, a former Edwards Lifesciences (EW) executive, as a meaningful upgrade to lead the company's next phase of growth.

Wingstop, a quick-service restaurant franchisor, reported quarterly results that exceeded expectations, but shares declined due to broader weakness across the restaurant industry. We remain confident in key growth initiatives, including expanded national advertising and the rollout of smart kitchen technology. Early results showed this smart kitchen technology has reduced customer service wait times by 40% within weeks of implementation, improving operational efficiency and enhancing the customer experience at minimal cost to franchisees. We are also optimistic about the planned 2026 launch of Wingstop's loyalty program, designed to enhance customer engagement and encourage repeat business. We added to our position during the quarter.

Portfolio Activity

During the quarter, we initiated new positions in Teledyne Technologies (TDY) and Waystar. Teledyne provides advanced sensing, transmission and analysis technologies across niche markets, including digital imaging, instrumentation, and aerospace and defense electronics. Its aerospace and defense segment is benefiting from rising global military spending, which is driving demand for high-performance sensors, drones and space systems. Flagship products like nano drones and proprietary sensor technologies position Teledyne well to meet evolving defense needs. The company's industrial markets are also recovering, supported by trends in automation and testing. Teledyne has consistently generated strong free cash flow, and management has a proven track record of value creation through disciplined M&A. With accelerating defense demand and a rebound in industrial markets, we see potential for mid-teens EPS growth driven by both organic growth and strategic expansion.

Waystar is a leading cloud-based revenue cycle management platform that supports over 30,000 health care organizations, including 16 of the top 20 US hospitals. We initiated a position based on our view that Waystar is well positioned to benefit from the fallout of last year's cyberattack on its largest competitor, Change Healthcare. While provider switching takes time, early signs indicate this shift is underway. Additionally, we believe Waystar stands to gain from integrating AI capabilities into its software, delivering measurable ROI by automating denial management and prior authorizations, helping providers overcome staffing constraints and improve claims performance.

In addition to Wingstop, we also added to RBC Bearings (RBC) and Parsons (PSN) during the quarter. RBC Bearings is a leading provider of specialty bearings, gearing and motion control products, with over 70% of revenue derived from sole- or primary-sourced components—underscoring its critical role in customer supply chains. Its strategy of producing ahead of demand supports strong delivery performance and quality, while proprietary design software enhances product engineering. We added to our position during the quarter, as we believe the company is well positioned to benefit from the production ramp-up of next-generation aircraft, supported by long-term contracts with Boeing (BA) and Airbus (OTCPK:EADSY), as well as steady aftermarket demand for maintenance and repair. The company recently issued a positive outlook, supported by a strong backlog and the successful integration of its VACCO acquisition, both of which signal continued growth potential.

Parsons is a technology-driven defense, intelligence and infrastructure engineering firm recognized as a leader in cybersecurity, missile defense and critical infrastructure protection for government and commercial clients. We believe the company is well positioned to capitalize on multiple secular growth drivers—including rising global defense spending, increased North American infrastructure investment fueled by the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, expanding infrastructure development in the Middle East as economies diversify beyond oil, and long-term opportunities in per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances remediation. We believe Parsons remains early in its growth journey relative to more established peers and is poised to deliver high-single-digit to low-double-digit organic revenue growth over the next 3–5 years, with modest margin expansion supporting a mid-teens compound annual growth rate in earnings per share.

We ended our investment campaigns in Atlassian and Bright Horizons (BFAM) during the quarter. Atlassian is a leading provider of team collaboration software. We believe the company's recent results reinforce the rationale behind our decision to exit the position. While cloud revenue growth remains healthy at 26% year-over-year, the deceleration from prior quarters suggests limited near-term acceleration in cloud migration. Ongoing concerns about Atlassian's seat-based pricing model, alongside broader uncertainty about the impact of AI on knowledge-based jobs, continue to weigh on sentiment. In our view, Atlassian must evolve from its traditional SaaS model toward a more usage-based or value-based monetization approach. However, this strategic shift lacks strong valuation support

unless executed rapidly and effectively. Our evolving perspective on SaaS fundamentals over the past six months, combined with the belief that meaningful upside is unlikely until its AI investments begin delivering substantial revenue, ultimately led us to fully exit the position.

Bright Horizons is a leading provider of employer-sponsored childcare and dependent care, offering full-service centers and flexible backup care well suited to hybrid work environments. We initiated our position in December 2024, anticipating a return-to-office catalyst. With over 60% of earnings from its scalable backup care business and underutilized centers expected to benefit from rising enrollments, we saw long-term potential. However, despite early signs of recovery, enrollment growth has remained modest and uneven, particularly in younger classrooms. Continued macro uncertainty and delayed office returns have created near-term visibility challenges, leading us to exit the position.

In addition to London Stock Exchange, notable trims in the quarter included Tyler Technologies and Clearwater Analytics (CWAN). Tyler Technologies provides end-to-end information management solutions and services for local government offices. When we purchased the stock in 2020, our investment thesis focused on its potential for durable growth, driven by the resilience of its end markets and the opportunity presented by its transition to cloud-based subscriptions. As anticipated, the shift to the cloud has accelerated revenue growth and contributed to margin expansion. We trimmed our position during the quarter as the stock's valuation increasingly reflected these benefits, but we continue to hold it in the Crop.

Clearwater Analytics provides cloud-based investment accounting, compliance and risk reporting solutions for insurers, corporations and asset managers. Recently, the company shifted its strategy from focusing on organic growth to pursuing larger scale M&A, deploying significant capital and taking on leverage. This pivot was not part of our original investment thesis, so we trimmed our position. Given the high quality of the franchise, we continue to monitor it closely for evidence that the company can successfully execute this new strategy.

Perspective

Similar to last quarter, solid earnings among our holdings helped drive positive absolute returns in Q3. If anything, we believe the fundamental business momentum of our Crop SM holdings strengthened through the summer. Our investment process leads us to concentrate capital in businesses possessing franchise characteristics, trading at reasonable valuations and benefiting from durable profit cycles. To see so many of our holdings report upbeat earnings during a period of significant macro uncertainty was gratifying.

Even more important, in our view, was the prospect of a significant change in the fortunes of the health care sector. In recent years, the portfolio's sector positions posted decent absolute returns but were meaningful laggards in this mega-cap tech rally. The sector's drag on relative performance was compounded by the portfolio's significant above-benchmark exposure. Regulatory uncertainty, tariffs and pricing pressures have been significant headwinds for the sector. However, favorable policy developments this quarter may indicate the start of a clearing event for health care stocks. If these developments continue to move in a positive direction, they would lift a large overhang that could set the stage for a sector revaluation with plenty of room to grow.

We've remained patient, however, not based on a top-down industry view but due to our high conviction in a select group of franchises. For example, companies such as Ascendis, Argenx and Insmed have been launching breakthrough medicines into large markets. Those launches are gaining momentum and poised to transform the financial profile of these franchises. And we believe West Pharmaceuticals, after a difficult post-COVID hangover period, is beginning to experience accelerating growth as a critical packaging supplier for most newly approved medicines. So while we continue to closely monitor industry policy developments, our conviction in these holdings remains high.

IT has been another pain point. Sector leadership has been unusually narrow, with outsized gains concentrated in a handful of names, most notably NVIDIA (NVDA), with elevated valuations that are contingent on the boom in AI investment. However, we believe the portfolio is on its way to resolving the most challenging sector headwinds. Over the past year, and particularly within the past quarter, we have started to gain a clearer perspective on what is working—and not working—in technology and AI. Our evolving perspective has guided a strategic shift in portfolio holdings. Businesses, such as Pure Storage, Lattice Semiconductor and Monolithic Power Systems, have demonstrated strong performance and alignment with AI trends. We have increased our conviction in these names. Meanwhile, SaaS-based software businesses tied to seat-based models, such as Atlassian and Sage Group (OTCPK:SGPYY), have faced mounting pressure from rapid advancements in large language models. These pressures are also mounting for companies in other industries that provide online and data services, such as London Stock Exchange Group. We've reduced exposure or exited these positions as they look increasingly vulnerable to disintermediation by AI.

Finally, segments of the financials sector have delivered outsized runs over the past few quarters. The trend began from a low base and has steadily gained momentum, aided by a combination of resilient credit fundamentals, robust demand for new issuance and narrowing credit spreads. We are willing to accept that there are times when certain portions of the market are stylistically inconsistent with our investment philosophy.

Downside protection remains an important component of our strategy. Despite the equity market's push higher in Q3, we still view the environment as unusual. Investors continue to look past a host of risks, including signs of consumer and labor market weakness, tariff uncertainty, volatile geopolitical events and erratic US government policy decisions—all of which occur against a backdrop of historically elevated market valuations. Speculative early-stage growth companies tied to popular themes such as quantum computing, cryptocurrencies and space have seen dramatic increases in their market valuations. The apparent disconnect between risks and behavior underscores the importance of discipline. As always, we believe our commitment to business fundamentals and valuation will once again help us to take advantage of volatility, which even in a rising market, never feels far away. In a landscape of shifting sentiment and mixed macro signals, we remain confident that staying grounded will continue to serve our portfolio well.

Investment Process

We seek to invest in companies that possess franchise characteristics, are benefiting from an accelerating profit cycle and are trading at a discount to our estimate of private market value. Our investment process focuses on two distinct elements—security selection and capital allocation. We overlay our investment process with broad knowledge of the global economy.

Security Selection

We seek to identify companies that have franchise characteristics (e.g., low-cost production capability, possession of a proprietary asset, dominant market share or a defensible brand name), are benefiting from an accelerating profit cycle and are trading at a discount to our estimate of private market value. We also assess governance and other material sustainability factors that could impact future stock returns. We look for companies that are well positioned for long-term growth, which is driven by demand for their products and services, at an early enough stage in their profit cycle to benefit from the increased cash flows produced by the emerging profit cycle.

Capital Allocation

Based on our fundamental analysis of a company's profit cycle, we divide the portfolio into three parts. Garden SM investments are small positions in the early part of their profit cycle that may warrant more sizeable allocations as their profit cycle accelerates. Crop SM investments are positions that are being increased to a full weight because they are moving through the strongest part of their profit cycles. Harvest SM investments are positions that are being reduced as they near our estimates of full valuation or their profit cycles begin to decelerate.

Broad Knowledge

We overlay the security selection and capital allocation elements of our investment process with a desire to invest opportunistically across the entire global economy. We seek broad knowledge of the global economy in order to find growth wherever it occurs.

Team Overview

We believe deep industry expertise, broad investment knowledge, a highly collaborative decision-making process and individual accountability are a powerful combination. Since the inception of the team, we have been committed to building a team of growth investors that retains these attributes and is solely dedicated to our process and approach.

data.title: RFI: Consistent Dividends But Underperforms Peer Funds (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Overview

As interest rates remain elevated, the real estate sector continues to lag behind the rest of the market. As a result, this can be a great time for investors to build positions in high quality real estate positions. However, there are hundreds of REITs available to investors and this makes it difficult to determine which may be a good opportunity. This is why funds like Cohen & Steers Total Return Realty (RFI) are attractive because it makes the process easy by enabling investors to receive instant diversification across a wide variety of sub-sectors across the real estate industry.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that RFI's share price has declined by about 9.66%. Even when including all distributions that were paid out, the total return still sits around a loss of 2.16% over the same time frame. Following the recent market pullback, RFI now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 8.5% and issues payouts on a monthly basis. Despite the fund keeping payouts the same for an extended period of time, I remain cautious about the overall sustainability of payouts if the macroenvironment for the real estate sector does not improve.

At the time of my last coverage, RFI traded at a premium to NAV of 3.89%. Due to the recent pullback, RFI now trades at a very small discount to NAV of approximately 0.18%. For reference, RFI has traded at an average premium to NAV of 3.25% over the last five year period. Despite the fund now trading in the discount territory, I do not believe that this automatically makes it a great time to accumulate shares. To help visualize the current discount to NAV pricing in relation to its historical range, we can reference the red line on the graph below.

When I previously covered RFI, I issued a buy rating due to the attractive valuation at the time. Despite the valuation becoming more attractive in recent months, I have decided to downgrade my rating on RFI after comparing the fund to some more enticing peers. Cohen & Steers has several real estate focused funds that provide a similar holdings exposure, but also outperforms RFI over a longer holding period. RFI offers no standout feature that can lead to outperformance during bull markets or resilience during market declines. So let's start by taking a look at the strategy RFI implements to generate its income.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, RFI now has total net assets of $311M that is spread across 178 different holdings. RFI also has a management fee of 0.90% of assets, which may be worth it considering the high yield that investors are receiving. The fund aims to provide attractive total returns through its portfolio of diverse real estate securities.

Occasionally, the fund will also write options against some of its holdings as a way to amplify the income that is generated from its positions. This can limit the growth potential of the fund but it seems like management keeps its option writing to a minimum. As we can see below, options are only being written against a select few positions.

What I like about RFI is that the fund provides instant diversity across the many sub-sectors of real estate. As we can see below, RFI is most heavily allocated to Healthcare investments at 15% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Telecommunication REITs and Corporate Bonds, which accounts for 13% and 10% of assets respectively. The fund also utilizes a blend between common stocks and preferred securities, which helps the fund demonstrate resilience through unfavorable market environments.

  • Common Stocks: 81%
  • Preferred & fixed Income: 19%

When looking closer into the positions within RFI, we can see that the fund provides exposure to some of the highest quality REITs. For instance, the largest position in Welltower (WELL) at 10.5% of assets. WELL has recently delivered strong earnings and the share price has now appreciated more than 58% on a YTD basis. I outlined the strength of this REIT in a recently published article, which I would recommend if you are interested in learning about why RFI may have chosen this as its largest position. This is followed by exposure to other high quality names such as American Tower Corp (AMT), Digital Realty Trust (DLR), Crown Castle (CCI), and Prologis (PLD), just to name a few.

Performance Risk

Despite the fund having exposure to so many high quality REITs, I believe that investors should limit their expectation around the fund's ability to provide attractive price growth over a longer holding period. For reference, we can see below that RFI's share price has declined by about 7.6% over the last ten year period. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 107.2% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of their returns will come from the fund's ability to continue paying out a high rate of distributions over time.

However, I believe that RFI is one of the least attractive real estate funds within Cohen & Steers' umbrella. While these funds may have different strategies when it comes to position selection, sector exposure, or leverage, the underlying premise of focusing on real estate holdings remain similar. I believe that the two most suitable peers are the following funds:

  • Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (RQI): ~8.2% dividend yield.
  • Cohen & Steers REIT & Preferred Income Fund (RNP): ~8% dividend yield.

As we can see below, RFI has the longest operating history but also has the lowest base assets under management. Conversely, RQI has the highest level of base assets under management at $1.68B, but also has the highest listed expense ratio. One of the main differentiator of RQI is the strategic use of leverage, which has helped the fund generate earnings that have more consistently supported distributions. I highlight the fund's performance in my most recent article on RQI. RNP differentiates itself by putting a greater emphasis on preferred securities, which makes up nearly 50% of its assets.

When running a performance comparison between this peer group, we can see that RFI has the lowest total return at 107.2% over the last ten years. In comparison, RQI's total return of 156.7% significantly outperforms the peer group by a wide margin. Since the three of these funds generally share a lot of the same holdings, it becomes challenging to recommend a position in RFI when there are more compelling peers out there.

Even when comparing the performance of these peers through market declines, RFI doesn't offer any significant benefits. A good example of this can be demonstrated by measuring the performance throughout 2022's market decline. As we can see below, RNP was the most resilient through this period of declines, which can likely be attributed to the greater emphasis on preferred securities and income generation.

Dividend Utility

One of the most important aspects for investors to understand is that the high yield comes at the cost of some tax consequences that must be considered. However, the distributions received from a real estate focused fund may not produce as large of a tax burden as you would expect. For instance, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that the majority of the distributions paid YTD were fueled by net realized long-term gains, which have favorable tax treatment for investors. Conversely, 38.94% of the distributions paid YTD were funded by unfavorable tax methods, including net investment income and net realized short-term capital gains.

While this indicates that the distributions are mostly fueled in a tax-efficient manner, this doesn't mean that the income received will be completely tax-free. So if you're an investor that wants to limit the overall tax liability received from RFI, a position would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account. Based on the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.08 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 8.5%. Looking at the payout history below, we can see that RFI has managed to keep payouts within a consistent range over the last five years.

While I wouldn't anticipate any sort of base distribution raise until earnings improve and interest rates are cut, I believe that this consistency should still be commended. The consistent payouts essentially allow shareholders to create their own dividend growth by reinvesting payouts and adding new capital on a continuous basis. My only concern is that we don't know how much longer RFI will struggle to generate earnings that can support payouts. The latest semi-annual earnings amount to $0.46 per share, which isn't enough to support the annual payout of $0.96 per share.

$0.08 monthly payout x 12 months = $0.96 per share annual payout.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am downgrading my rating on RFI as a hold at this time. Despite the fund's attractive price to NAV valuation, the fund underperforms some more enticing peers that Cohen & Steers have available, such as RQI and RNP. Despite the underperformance against peers, I don't think this automatically means that existing investors should sell out of their position. While performance can always improve, I think the fund is still a solid choice for investors seeking a consistent source of income generation.

data.title: Kyndryl: Revenue Trade-Off Drives Margins, Stock Remains Deeply Undervalued
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The second quarter of fiscal year 2026 was expected to be average in terms of financial results. The market reacted unnecessarily negatively to the drop in revenue, in my opinion. In the first article, I wrote that the main revenue growth should be expected from the third quarter onwards in the fourth quarter, while the trend towards margin growth will remain in the second quarter. And since my article was published, the stock has fallen by almost 19% more. This makes it possible to buy very oversold shares of a good company, which is gradually coping with the problems that hinder revenue growth. I'm betting on revenue growth in the second half of the year compared to 2025. So far, everything is going according to my scenario, so I'm not changing the Buy rating. At the same time, I have added conservatism to my DCF model, so the target price of the stock will be lowered from $36 to $30 with an upside of 24%.

Second-Quarter Trade-Off: Revenue Light, Profit Heavy

The final period of restructuring and adaptation of Kyndryl Holdings (KD) after the separation from IBM is relatively painless for financial results – revenue has practically stopped falling. But the highly competitive market in which the company operates does not let you relax, so management is constantly reviewing the contract portfolio and improving all areas of its activities. I analyzed the company's business in detail in the first article – I recommend reading it before continuing to read this article.

As for the reporting, in my opinion, the negative was excessive. Yes, revenue fell by 1% YoY, which did not meet our forecasts with the market – I still expected +0-1% growth. But it's too early to draw conclusions, we need to understand the root causes of this decline. The fact is that the company deliberately decided to sacrifice revenue in favor of marginality this quarter. To do this, the entire contract portfolio was reviewed and cleared of low-margin contracts. Management claims that if the contract portfolio had not been touched, then most likely revenue would have increased slightly in Q2. I readily believe this, as the margin has really increased. More importantly, signings will continue to remain above revenue, despite a slight drop in YoY, which indicates the stability of orders. Revenues from consulting and partnerships with hyperscalers are also growing well.

The strategy to increase profitability has worked well in principle. Cleaning the portfolio and automating processes allowed us to earn $98 million in net profit before taxes in Q2 2026 against a loss in Q2 2025. Without one-time adjustments, growth was even higher - $123 million, and adjusted net profit was about $89 million (EPS of about $0.38). Non-GAAP EPS increased from $0.01 to $0.38. As for EBITDA, I slightly overestimated the company's capabilities, expecting that the EBITDA margin would reach 18%. In fact, EBITDA increased by 15%, and marginality was 17%. But here, rather, I was overly positive.

After the publication of the report, the shares immediately fell by 4%. Investors didn't like the drop in revenue. But I think it's a mistake to focus only on the movement of one indicator without understanding the context as a whole. Looking deeper into what is happening, it is clear that the decline in revenue was conscious and manageable – it was sacrificed for the sake of margin growth. If the margin had not increased or had grown slightly, then I would have agreed with the revaluation of the shares down, but in fact the management's bet worked – revenue fell slightly, and marginality increased significantly. This is essentially a continuation of the announced "Alliances, Advanced Delivery, Accounts" initiative - the entire portfolio is being reviewed, AI automation is being introduced, and margins are growing. For me, it is much more important what will happen in the third quarter, and it will be clear from the results of the third quarter whether my bid will work or not.

Eyes on Q3: Revenue Rebound, Margin Held

The guidance has not changed. The CEO of the company confirmed my expectations that weak revenue results for the first half of the year will be offset by growth in the second half of the year. I also haven't revised down my expectations for the third quarter.

In my forecast for the third quarter, revenue will increase by 2-3% year-on-year, adjusted for currency, and adjusted EBITDA margin will exceed 18%. There is every reason to fulfill this plan – a large backlog, high growth rates of the consulting segment and partnerships with hyperscalers. I also think that management will not sacrifice growth for margins as much as it did this quarter.

DCF Reset: More Conservative Assumptions, Still Undervalued

In this chapter, I will talk about my DCF model, its prerequisites, and final evaluation. Let me remind you that I calculate the target share price by forecasting the unlevered FCF for the period 2026-2030, discount the forecast FCFS, then calculate the terminal value by multiplying the forward EV/EBITDA and P/E by EBITDA and net profit of the last forecast year (2030) and also discount. I add up the discounted FCF and Terminal Value to get the total value of the business (when calculated using P/E, this is immediately the Market cap. with EV/EBITDA, this is the Enterprise value from which Net Debt is also subtracted), which I divide by the number of shares and get the projected fair value of one share.

After the results of the second quarter, I made the prerequisites of the financial model more conservative, which led to a decrease in the fair share price. In the new model, the revenue CAGR for 2026-2030 will be 2.44% compared to 3.15% in the previous model. The logic of this premise is based on the fact that signatures will continue to remain above revenue, but will not grow much. Management will clear the portfolio of old low-margin contracts and gradually increase the EBIT margin to 7-8% by 2030. CapEx will be maintained at 4.5-5% of revenue, and net working capital will require only minimal investments (about 0.5% of sales per year). Thus, the average FCF Yield in 2026-2030 will be 6.23% - for comparison, according to the results of the previous model, the average FCF Yield was about 10%.

WACC decreased from 8.54% to 7.76% due to a reduction in equity's share in the company's capital from 64.2% to 57.8% (and the cost of equity significantly exceeds the cost of debt for the company). This was due to falling stock prices and a corresponding drop in the market cap.

Kyndryl trades at huge discounts to its sector peers. And this discount continues to increase. Obviously, the stock is too oversold. It feels like the company is in danger of default – its multipliers are so low. This is a clear inefficiency – the market is overly skeptical of Kyndryl. All because of falling revenue. But it seems that the turning point of this trend is near and soon we will see a gradual slow growth in revenue. This should revalue the stock price upwards. Shares should be worth at least 20-30% more expensive.

A more conservative model obviously gives lower target prices than in the previous model. But prices were also supported by a reduction in WACC. Thus, in the baseline scenario, both valuation methods give almost the same forecast of $30-31 with an upside of 24-27%. The model proves a clear underestimation of stocks. The lower the WACC and the higher the multiplier, the stronger the upside, reaching almost 50%. And there is no downside at all – in the worst-case scenario, the shares should cost 11-13% more.

Management Allocates Capital Where It Matters Most

The gradual reduction of the debt burden allowed the company to start paying shareholders. The company does this through buybacks. By the end of September, Kyndryl had purchased 7.3 million shares (approximately 3% of the securities) worth $249 million. In the Q2, the company spent $89 million, buying back an additional 2.9 million shares. This is a competent approach on the part of management, which I support. In the current environment, buybacks are the right decision, as shares are too cheap and can be redeemed, spending less money and maintaining the share price and EPS at the same time. Acquisitions in Q2 occurred at a weighted average price of around $30, higher than current quotations. This means that, perhaps, in the third quarter, the company will allocate even more funds for buybacks – I think about $ 100 million.

Risks

The biggest risk is a drop in revenue. All this time, stocks have been getting cheaper precisely because of the annual drop in revenue. If management fails to reverse the course of events, then the shares will fall in price even more. This risk can be strongly influenced by another risk – high competition. The largest IT companies such as Accenture, IBM Consulting, large vendors and cloud providers are striving to occupy this niche. And this can lead to a loss of market share and, consequently, a drop in revenue. And in order to maintain market share, it may be necessary to lower prices, which will hit margins. The debt burden, although manageable, is high, which limits the company's flexibility and payments to shareholders.

Final Thoughts

At the moment, this is an interim article – the success or failure of this investment idea will be clear sooner after the publication of the report for the third quarter. So far, I see a clear underestimation in the shares of a good company that is gradually breaking out of a difficult situation and getting its business back on track. The Buy rating is saved. I will write the next article about Kyndryl in January.

data.title: The Most Dangerous Economic Experiment Of My Lifetime, And The Only Way I Know To Win It
data.description:
Introduction

It’s time to talk about the economy, after we spent a lot of time discussing the market, AI bubble risks, and where I’m putting my money. We’ll do some of that today as well, with the major difference that we’re going to put a lot more energy into diving into critical economic developments that not only impact the fabric of our societies but also our portfolios.

It’s one of these articles that touches on a wide range of developments, including the massive AI revolution, which I called "The Biggest Market Disruption Since The Industrial Revolution" in the title of a September 6 article.

In that article, I brought up Leopold Aschenbrenner, a German-born "AI influencer" whom I consider one of the smartest minds in the industry. He now runs an AI-focused hedge fund in the San Francisco Bay Area with at least $1.5 billion in assets as of last month.

He wrote a fantastic paper last year titled "Situational Awareness," which focused on the massive benefits of AI, its innovative power, and the many (super serious) risks tied to an AI-driven world. I used it in my September 6 article. Now, I’m bringing it up again as I spend more time on it. Especially this part stood out to me:

AI progress won’t stop at human-level. Hundreds of millions of AGIs could automate AI research, compressing a decade of algorithmic progress (5+ OOMs) into ≤1 year. We would rapidly go from human-level to vastly superhuman AI systems. The power - and the peril - of superintelligence would be dramatic. - Situational Awareness

In other words, while AI benefits may currently be rather limited, the progress is rapid and could soon result in decades of progress being achieved in less than a single year. That’s when AI gets superhuman intelligence. Essentially, it means that the price of intelligent thinking drops to almost zero, which could have substantial consequences for the labor market if you think about it.

However, it could also help us cure cancer, as rapid innovation could even target proteins in the body that we currently cannot treat. At some point, cancer could be a chronic disease instead of the deadly risk that so many face.

My point is that it feels like AI is either absolutely terrific or horrible, which depends entirely on what side of AI someone operates, works, and/or invests. As silly (or obvious) as this may sound, to me, it’s so serious that I decided to dedicate an entire article to it.

See, AI isn’t just an economic disruption, but it has massive implications for the central bank and policymakers, as it has completely messed up the economic cycle, creating a K-shaped economy that could force "unnatural" responses from the organizations in charge.

As I believe this comes with substantial risks, investors may be forced to make adjustments.

Luckily, there are plenty of those as well.

So, let’s dive in!

Why The Economy Is So Extremely Tricky

One thing I would have added to the introduction, if it weren’t so long already, is that this economy isn’t a normal economy. Using Ritholz's data below, we see that before the Second World War, the economy was in a recession roughly 40% of the time. Between the 1940s and the 2000s, that number hovered somewhere in the high-teens range.

Since the Great Financial Crisis, recessions seem to have vanished entirely, as we have had only two months of recession time in the U.S. since then. That was the brief recession when the economy was locked down due to the 2020 pandemic.

That’s it. Two months of recession time since 2008(!).

Interestingly enough, recessions aren’t gone. They are just really well hidden, as the economy and stock market have become extremely well diversified. For example, in 2015/2016, we were dealing with a manufacturing and commodity recession.

Since 2022, we’ve been dealing with a housing and transportation recession, which are two highly correlated industries with ties to consumer health and (global) trade.

The right word for this is "rolling recession." It impacts certain industries but isn’t bad enough to force the National Bureau of Economic Research to declare an official recession.

Right now, we seem to have rolling recessions and a K-shaped recovery, which is a situation where one group (of industries or consumers) does well, while another group suffers.

Morning Brew hit the nail on the head when it used four very prominent examples to explain why this K-shape is getting stronger, which is bad news for the economy in general.

  • It noted that the average new car in the U.S. now sells for more than $50,000. Meanwhile, repossessions were up 12% through the end of September.

On a side note, this is what delinquency rates among various loan types look like:

  • Delta Air Lines (DAL) noted that premium seat sales exceed coach seats for at least two quarters. I would have never guessed that if you had asked me.
  • Coca-Cola (KO) is seeing strong demand for premium brands like Smartwater, while its Coke brands are seeing growth at dollar stores.

Here’s a great quote they included:

Economic trends suggest that the gap will widen rather than narrow. Economist Betsey Stevenson of the University of Michigan told Marketplace, “The real risk to a K-shaped economy is social and political instability.” - Via Morning Brew

Jordi Visser, whom I have brought up in prior market/macro articles as well, discussed this in a recent video as well. His view perfectly confirms our own thesis, as he makes the case that the AI boom is creating both winners and losers, which means that society as a whole isn’t benefiting, at least not for now.

  • One group benefits from rising asset prices, AI-driven profit margins, and strong corporate earnings. After all, if the S&P 500 cuts labor costs and enhances general efficiencies, it can somewhat decouple itself from the economy as a whole.
  • Meanwhile, layoffs, inflation, and other factors impact loan quality, as I already briefly mentioned. This also impacts other areas, like consumer spending, and more.

The most straightforward example is Las Vegas, which is seeing weak traffic. Do you really like flying to Las Vegas to buy expensive drinks and food if you’re fearing for your job, unable to buy a home, and/or struggling with inflation?

He also agrees with another point I have brought up, which is that supporting weaker industries/consumers is a political priority. This applies to any administration, regardless of who’s in charge. If you’re in charge and the economy isn’t good, you’re likely in trouble.

Essentially, this could lead to a number of outcomes, including the need for economic handouts and unusual support. Right now, the two biggest examples are the new 50-year mortgage idea (it’s not a fact yet) and a $2,000 handout to people making less than six figures.

So far, these are just ideas, but I think they are excellent examples of where this is going. And, to be honest, these pieces perfectly fit the puzzle (thesis).

On November 15, The Wall Street Journal wrote about the need and difficulty for the Trump government to lower the cost of living.

In addition to the measures I just noted, it has even caused Trump to lower tariffs on key items that tend to quickly impact consumer prices:

Reducing tariffs is one of Trump’s most potent tools for lowering prices. On Friday, the administration said it would lower tariffs on beef, coffee, nuts, spices and dozens of other agricultural and food goods, marking a significant shift in Trump’s forceful approach to trade policy.

Prices for some food items have increased as a result of tariffs. The average price of a pound of ground roast coffee, for example, was $9.14 in September, according to the Labor Department, compared with $6.47 a year earlier. Much of that increase was because of a 50% tariff on certain imports from Brazil, one of the world’s largest coffee producers. - WSJ

Currently, President Trump is in a pickle, similar to President Biden. While inflation was higher under President Biden, both presidents struggled with the fact that it is very hard to quickly impact the economy through political measures. They also struggle with communication, as the AP explains. Just like Biden, Trump is making the case that inflation is no problem, while most consumers do feel that prices are very much an issue. That's politics.

That’s not a political statement. All I’m saying is that it’s hard for presidents to balance communication and measures to fight inflation. It’s tough and a problem for both parties, especially the one that’s in charge.

To make matters worse, as I wrote yesterday, everything is connected. AI, for example, is causing power inflation to pressure consumers, as the WSJ article I just brought up mentions:

Other prices could be harder to bring under control. Housing costs, for example, are high in large part as a result of a shortage of available homes, but alleviating that can’t be done quickly. Oil prices are already near their lowest levels in over four years, but electricity costs are on the rise as surging demand from data centers confronts an aging infrastructure. - WSJ

Then there’s the Fed (the money printer).

The Fed Is About To Become A Much Bigger Factor - And It’s A Dangerous Game

In prior articles, I have brought up that liquidity is becoming an issue in the shorter-term bond market, which could force the Fed to expand its balance sheet again (ending quantitative tightening and providing more liquidity).

Jordi Visser has a similar view, as he notes that the Fed has already ended tightening and is now forced to stimulate the economy to protect the "losers" of the K-shaped economy.

This could be inflationary due to more liquidity and offset the disinflationary tailwinds from accelerating AI adoption, at least for the time being.

It’s a point I have made before, albeit in a somewhat different context. In prior articles, like this one, I have explained that the Fed and government are likely to accept higher inflation to stimulate the economy. Essentially, they risk higher inflation to broaden growth, get the housing market unstuck, and even lower the real value of the national debt, as higher growth and inflation could lower the debt ratio.

It’s a dangerous game with both huge downsides and upsides. The downside is stagflation, as efforts could fail, leaving us with higher inflation and lower growth (or even a recession). The upside is terrific, as it could unlock the first durable economic expansion since 2016.

That’s when industrials, cyclicals, energy, housing, and so many others started a multi-year recovery. They did it in 2020 as well, but that one lasted less than two years.

This brings me to comments from Morgan Stanley, which did a podcast titled "Walking a Narrow Economic Path" on September 4. It’s been a couple of months, but what they said is still extremely valid.

They explained that markets are strong and financial conditions are loose. That’s still the case. They also noted that inflation is above the Fed’s target and moving higher. This is also true. Yet, job growth has collapsed. This is also true and historically very unusual.

That’s why they brought up the fine line between cutting rates into strength and weakness.

Historically speaking, if the Fed cuts into growth, the market tends to do extremely well, fueled by earnings growth tailwinds and more liquidity. Cutting into weakness (I like to call this "forced" cuts) suggests there’s something wrong with the economy. It tends to hurt medium-term earnings growth and capital gains.

It’s like seeing firefighters show up to your house when it’s burning. You’re glad they are there, but your house is still gone. I know it’s silly, but it’s the best thing I could come up with right now.

Now, it’s doing both at the same time, as AI is fueling a big part of the economy, while the weaker part basically forces the Fed to ease. I have said it before, and I still stand by it, which is why I am very happy I’m not in the shoes of the president or the Fed chairman.

As stock valuations are close to a multi-decade high and credit spreads (see below) are close to their long-term lows, injecting liquidity into the economy could cause it to overheat rather quickly.

As you can imagine, this can actually accelerate the K-shape, as it supports stock market multiples, AI spending, and credit demand.

That’s why government policy measures are so important, as capital needs to flow into the right areas, including housing, manufacturing, energy (AI bottlenecks), and similar.

The Fed cannot build homes, lower housing costs, deal with healthcare inflation, fix data center bottlenecks, or do other things. It controls the cost of money and can lower longer-term rates through intervention (creating more liquidity).

Implications For The Market

The worst part of this market is that, despite the risks, a "running it hot" strategy could force us into risk assets like equities if we want to avoid getting pressured by inflation.

Personally, I’m positioned for a successful outcome, as I invest in equities in cyclical value areas like housing, transportation, and energy. I buy the highest-quality stocks in these areas I can find, as I am taking the cyclical risks, but I’m not willing to take elevated company risks.

If we get bottoming cyclical growth, which I expect, we could see a rotation into these cheaper parts of the market, unlocking value in areas that haven’t done well in years. I believe this could create substantial alpha.

On a side note, the cyclical value model portfolio (this one is a paper account, although I own many of these stocks in my real account) is now down just 1.8%, as energy has started to rotate. These are just some ideas I like for this thesis:

However, there are also benefits for higher-yielding stocks like the ones that retirees like to buy and own. For example, as I wrote last week, I believe REITs are showing a terrific setup, which could get fueled by pressure on real rates (if longer-term bond rates are forced to come down in a high-inflation environment).

This could create a strong rotation into REITs, high-quality midstream, and even undervalued healthcare and consumer staple stocks.

In general, a successful move by the Fed and government can create more widespread earnings strength and rotate capital from the top to the bottom of the market.

That’s why I am so upbeat. Despite substantial risks in the economy and a top-heavy economy, there are opportunities that have been mostly ignored by large investors in recent years.

Moreover, by buying cyclical value, I’m essentially betting on the U.S. economy. I don’t care much who sits in the White House. What I care about is that my interests are aligned. If the American economy does well, my portfolio will likely generate significant alpha.

While that’s not always the best bet (it sure wasn’t in recent years), I love the odds at this point of the cycle, as I think we’ll see a load of support from the government, the Fed, and even AI innovation and adoption benefits.

That’s why I am rotating into these areas and deploying new capital.

While this may sound like a closing argument, here’s a quick summary of my thoughts:

Takeaway

I think we’re dealing with substantial economic and market challenges, as AI is accelerating the top of the economy, while the bottom is struggling. That’s now pressuring the White House and Fed to take action.

If this experiment works, it could be extremely bullish, as it could broaden economic growth and inject more liquidity into an economy that is, on average, not in a recession.

This creates opportunities for investors in both undervalued cyclical value stocks and high-quality income, as lower real rates and broadening growth should be terrific tailwinds for income.

The downside is elevated stagflation risks, which would be horrible news for the economy and market and likely trigger substantial underperformance in my portfolio. However, it’s a risk I feel I have to take, as the risk/reward is simply too good in my view.

data.title: Nationwide GQG U.S. Quality Equity Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Environment

During the third quarter, the S&P 500 index increased 8.12% with 10 of the 11 sectors exhibiting a positive return. The largest drivers of the benchmark’s gain were information technology (+13.2%, contributing 435 basis points (BPs) to the performance of the S&P 500 index), communication services (+12.0%, +120 bps), and consumer discretionary (+9.5%, +100 bps).

Stylistically, the S&P 500 Growth Index (9.8%) easily outperformed the S&P 500 Value index (6.2%) during the period.

In the US, we believe the positive return in the equity market was driven by three catalysts. First, it was another solid earnings season for the S&P 500 with aggregate earnings per share (EPS) growth in 2Q25, reported during 3Q25, of 12% versus the prior year.

Second, investors continued to express optimism about a higher and longer capital expenditure cycle to support data center construction to satisfy expected demand for generative artificial intelligence applications. During 3Q25, one of the large language model developers signed a massive multi-year contract for computing power with a cloud service provider. If both parties can perform on that agreement, that would be a tailwind for the semiconductor and electrification supply chains.

Third, a weakening job market improved the likelihood that the Federal Reserve would loosen monetary policy. Non-farm payroll growth in August was less than expected while the unemployment rate ticked up from 4.2% to 4.3%. In addition, for the 12 months ending March 2025, the number of jobs estimated by the Bureau of Labor Statistics was revised down by more than 900,000. That was the largest preliminary revision on record going back to 2000. In September, the Fed indeed lowered its overnight policy rate target by 25 bps to 4.00-4.25%, the central bank’s first rate reduction in nine months. The Fed chairman referred to the new policy target as modestly restrictive, implying that additional rate cuts are possible this year if inflation does not spike and the recent softness in the labor market continues.

Regarding trade policy, the Trump administration appears to be following a template of 10% baseline tariffs on countries with which the US has a trade surplus, 15% on those with which the US has a small deficit (including Japan and the EU), and higher tariffs on countries with which the US has a large trade deficit. However, the trade agreements announced to date are not concrete deals but frameworks. Critical details on the trade policies have yet to be resolved, in our view, as the US tries to limit the re-routing of Chinese exports through other countries, among other goals. Importantly, the US Supreme Court is expected to rule within a few months on the legality of the administration’s use of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act for certain tariffs.

The US 10-year Treasury yield entered the quarter at 4.23%, peaked in mid-July at 4.49%, and ended the period at 4.15%. Investment grade credit spreads in the US narrowed by ten bps in 3Q25 to 76 bps, while high-yield spreads tightened by 16 bps to 280 bps. The Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index (largely US Treasuries, investment grade corporates, and agency mortgage-backed securities) had a gain of 1.1% during the quarter. The US Dollar Index (DXY) strengthened 0.9% in the period.

The Bloomberg Commodity Index increased 2.6% during 3Q25 as gains in gold (+16.8%) and iron ore (+11.5%) were only partially offset by losses in wheat (-12.0%) and copper (-4.5%). Gold appears to be attractive as a safe-haven asset relative to the US dollar due to the country’s unpredictable trade policies, record debt levels, and the perception of waning central bank independence.

Portfolio Positioning

The utilities sector is the largest overweight in the portfolio with information technology the largest underweight. Headwinds include the potential unavailability of material and labor due to evolving trade and immigration policies from the Trump administration as the utility companies seek to add power generation, transmission, and distribution capacity, in our opinion. In addition, the sector faces regulatory risks including rate determinations and approvals of cap ex plans. Another potential headwind we believe is a continued increase in AI- related spending that could drive positive EPS revisions in the tech sector, which would likely impact our relative performance. We expect to monitor the relevant data closely and adapt to the changing environment.

We reduced our exposure to the information technology sector. We are significantly underweight tech as we believe many tech names are overearning due to the acceleration of AI-related capital expenditures from the providers of cloud services as they construct data centers to meet expected demand from the developers of large language models (LLMs). Regarding the sustainability of the current run rate of AI-related cap ex spending, we are skeptical. The proliferation of data center construction is likely to result in meaningful overcapacity of computing power, in our opinion, leading to declining cloud service pricing and poor economic returns. Also, we believe the LLMs are unlikely to generate the requisite subscription revenue from their user bases to pay for the computing power demand the LLMs are promising to the cloud service providers.

Finally, we think valuations still matter. Many tech stocks are expensive, in our view, with a risk of a quick, severe multiple compression if these companies lower their earnings guidance.

In contrast, we added exposure to certain names in the energy and consumer staples sectors as we found those opportunities more compelling on a risk-adjusted basis.

We do not target any specific factor exposures in the portfolio. We believe that backward-looking quantitative measures can give investors a false sense of diversification and precision. We prefer to focus on the potential of a permanent loss of capital by analyzing the end consumers of the businesses in which we invest and their behavior in various economic environments.

Outlook

We are overweight the Utilities sector. While the near-term supply of power in the US is expected to be static, we believe there are three catalysts driving an increase in electricity demand. First, the increasing use of generative AI applications is likely to require meaningful levels of computing power from data centers currently in operation, as well as those planned to open during the next several years. These data centers are expected to require additional power supplies. Second, certain areas of the country are benefiting from a migration in population. Third, we expect an increase in the onshoring of manufacturing plants in response to new trade policies in the US. As a result, we believe there is good visibility on annualized earnings growth for select utility names during the next five years.

We are also overweight Consumer Staples as we believe the fundamentals for certain tobacco businesses are solid with growth coming from next generation products. In addition, select tobacco stocks have what we consider to be attractive dividend yields. We are constructive on opportunities in the Financials sector as well. Certain property and casualty insurers have high recurring revenue, in our opinion, and the potential to mine their large data sets with AI applications to price risk more efficiently going forward.

We are underweight the Information Technology sector. We are skeptical that the current run rate of AI-related cap ex spending is sustainable and believe certain tech names are overearning. In addition, many stocks in the sector are expensive, in our opinion, with a risk of compression in their valuation multiples.

Headwinds include the potential unavailability of material and labor due to evolving trade and immigration policies from the Trump administration as the utility companies seek to add power generation, transmission, and distribution capacity. In addition, the sector faces regulatory risks including rate determinations and approvals of cap ex plans. Another potential headwind is a continued increase in AI-related spending that could drive positive EPS revisions in the tech sector, which would likely impact our relative performance.

While we are not economists, we do monitor macro data as part of our risk framework. We underwrite the existing, and potential, companies in our portfolios focusing on the visibility of their future free cash flows. Ignoring the macro conditions in which these companies operate would impact the accuracy of any investor’s earnings outlook, in our opinion. However, adapting our portfolios to reflect new information is a hallmark of our process and we will continue to go where the data leads us.
